In stage 2 the model plays and the simulator scores; there are no gold actions. Games are batched (one game per training sequence, many independent games per batch) and the update is score-weighted cross-entropy on the model's own trajectories, not policy-gradient reinforcement learning.

Per-step scores (net change of position value) plus per-game outcomes weight the model's own played trajectories, which are then trained on with the ordinary next-token loss, so the outcome-driven signal flows through the stable operator already in use. Sequential game structure in the context window is what trains state tracking: decisions late in a game condition on everything earlier, including the model's own moves. A replay fraction of stage-1 text in every batch anchors language at the gradient level.

Compute topology from measured numbers: training consumes about 30k tokens per second at mini scale; listener and rendering calls are short, so roughly two to four LLM GPUs per training GPU behind an asynchronous buffer keep training unblocked, and the dominant multiplier is the keep rate, which score-weighting (rather than hard outcome filtering) holds at one. Generation already consumes 8-16 GPUs in the existing pipeline, so this is a reallocation of the power-hungry step, not a new cost. Decisions are reason-bearing: the model must say what to do and why, and the listener will not act on a bare decision without a reason attached, though it does not judge the reason and acts even on flawed reasoning, so the format is soft pressure rather than a hard gate. Beyond that single reason clause, the format reserves extended working text before the decision, disabled initially for interpretability: a forward pass can chain roughly as many dependent steps as the model has layers, and emitted intermediate tokens are the only way past that ceiling, so enabling the reserved space later is a measurable capability intervention at matched token budgets.
